text.fullText:
RE: Docket ID EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929, Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations

I strongly oppose the Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act, Section 401, Water Quality Certifications. For more than 50 years, Section 401 of the Clean Water Act has been a critical tool that enables states and the federal government to cooperatively manage our shared water resources, that ensures local voices are regarded in the process, and that safeguards processes resulting in stronger, more durable projects. The proposed rulemaking is contrary to the language of the Clean Water Act and would greatly restrict states’ and Tribes’ long-standing authority to protect waters within their borders.

SCOPE OF CERTIFICATION AUTHORITY. The proposed rule narrows the scope of states’ and Tribes’ certification authority to water quality-related conditions that may be applied only to the discharge, not to the project as a whole.

Water quality certification is one of the most important and effective tools available to states and Tribes to ensure federally licensed projects are protective of water quality for drinking and recreation and as aquatic habitat for fish and wildlife. The proposed rule would greatly undermine state and Tribal authority to protect their waters by restricting the type of protective conditions states and Tribes can put on federally licensed or permitted activities that threaten state or Tribal waters.

States and Tribes must be able to…
> Review oil and gas infrastructure, data centers, hydropower projects, roads, etc. that could violate their water-quality standards.
> Consider spill risks, threats to cultural sites, and impacts on fish and wildlife when weighing in on federal projects.
> Evaluate infrastructure that could cause higher water temperatures and habitat degradation that would be detrimental to fish and wildlife, as well as a loss of recreation and clean, flowing water for communities.
> Protect the waters within their borders, which has been their long-standing authority.

TIMING AND INFORMATION REQUIREMENTS. The proposed rule establishes strict timeline and information requirements for states and Tribes to consider a request for certification. States and Tribes must have adequate time and information to review and process complex applications and the ability to impose conditions on permits that will protect water quality, especially given the differences in types of resources and water-quality standards among states.

IN CONCLUSION. In 2023, the EPA appropriately reasserted the strong role that states and Tribes should have in protecting their waters. That role—which has been the norm for most of the last 55 years—must not be weakened. I urge you to withdraw the proposed rule and ask that you uphold the longstanding authority of states and Tribes to protect waters within their boundaries.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment.
